title: Objective-C And Swift
description: ObjC and Swift Mixed Programming Practice: Bridging Headers, Umbrella Headers, Access Decorating, and Library Evolution Configuration.
content:
In 2020, I started migrating some work projects to Swift. This document compiles some issues encountered during the migration and their solutions.

## Objective-C and Swift Mixed Programming: Official Explanation

[Importing Objective-C into Swift](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swift/imported_c_and_objective-c_apis/importing_objective-c_into_swift)

[Importing Swift into Objective-C](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swift/imported_c_and_objective-c_apis/importing_swift_into_objective-c)

### Cocoa Touch Framework does not support Bridge Header

When thinking about mixed programming, people often think of Bridge Headers. However, the Framework does not support Bridge Headers.

For simplicity, we will directly point out the viewpoint specifically mentioned in the official documentation.

#### Importing Objective-C into Swift

>“To import a set of Objective-C files in the same framework target as your Swift code, you’ll need to import those files into the Objective-C... umbrella header for the framework.
>
>To import Objective-C code into Swift from the same framework
>
>Under Build Settings, in Packaging, make sure the Defines Module setting for that framework target is set to “Yes”.
In your umbrella header file, import every Objective-C header you want to expose to Swift. For example:”

#### Importing Swift into Objective-C

>To import a set of Swift files in the same framework target as your Objective-C code, you don’t need to import anything into the umbrella header for the framework. Instead, import the Xcode-generated header file for your Swift code into any Objective-C .m file you want to use your Swift code from.
>
>Because the generated header for a framework target is part of the framework’s public interface, only declarations marked with the public or open modifier appear in the generated header for a framework target.”

What needs special attention here is the .m keyword.

Swift code was introduced in the ObjC project, The reason why related header files must be included in the .m file is simple: to avoid circular references to the same header file. This is because the XXX-Swift.h imported in the Module is already in a public state, essentially exporting some Objective-C files again.

There are actually two solutions:

1. Use the .m file mentioned in the book. Place the imported XXX-Swift.h in the .m file imported into Objective-C to reduce exposure to the .h file and avoid publicization.

2. If you are using CocoaPods and configuring your project through Podspec, manually filter out the relevant public files, and then keep other files in Project state by default (the reverse is also true for Private). If you are using Xcode Project management, simply modify the access permissions.

> Source: Xcode Developer Library > Tools & Languages ​​> IDEs > Project Editor Help > Setting the Visibility of a Header File

## Xcode Configuration Preparation

If your project is a ProjectDemo For a project, configure a framework. For mixed-language projects, both configurations must support Swift.

For example, the ProjectDemo project needs to configure the projectName-Bridging-Header.h configuration file and import a .swift file (empty is acceptable).

For the framework, any Objective-C header files imported by Swift from other projects must be included in the umbrella file for import. Additionally, imported files must be public.

Only then can compilation be considered complete. In short, if any of the following frameworks in your project use Swift, even if the project itself doesn't use Swift code, you still need to enable the Swift configuration.

If your project

### Pure Swift Project with Objective-C project build

Build for Swift Project

1. Open your Xcode, select your build target,`Build Settings`, open `Build Libraries for Distribution`, switch to `YES` (Pure Swift Project need support LibraryEvolution [Library Evolution for Stable ABIs](https://github.com/apple/swift-evolution/blob/master/proposals/0260-library-evolution.md) ) [WWDC](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2019/416/).
2. Need at `@objc` if you want share to Objective-C.
3. Support Objective-C access. `Open`, `public`, `internal`, `fileprivate`, `private`

#### Note: Enable Library Evolution

> The legacy build system does not support building projects with Swift when SWIFT_ENABLE_LIBRARY_EVOLUTION is enabled.

```console
<unknown>:0: error: module compiled with Swift 5.2.4 cannot be imported by the Swift 5.1.2 compiler
```

Setting BUILT_PRODUCTS_DIR is On

### Modular Headers

Xcode config

1. Defines Module(DEFINES_MODULE), Switch `YES`
2. Module Map File(MODULEMAP_FILE). If use by LLVM control this file, will automatic file, otherwise by hand setting.

CocoaPods Config

Custom config your CocoaPods file, like podfile, podspce.

1. podspce config, insert `DEFINES_MODULE' => 'YES` at `pod_target_xcconfig`. eg: s.pod_target_xcconfig = { 'DEFINES_MODULE' => 'YES' }
2. podfile, open all pod config modular. `use_modular_headers!`
3. special pod config on podfile. eg: :`modular_headers => true`## Some Tips for Project Practice

### Reflection

```objc
[NSClassFromString(@"ViewController") alloc] initWithNib:nil withBundle:nil];
```

```swift
// 依靠Mirro, 待补充

```

### Swift import

The following method is used to resolve situations where files cannot be found due to internal imports within the SDK or imports from the pod's source code.

```objc
#if __has_include("TargetName-Swift.h")
    #import "TargetName-Swift.h"
#else
    #import <TargetName/TargetName-Swift.h>
#endif
```In larger projects, adding the above four lines of code each time isn't cumbersome. However, if related refactoring or modifications are needed later, it will bring extra work. Therefore, for my work, I introduced a new header file specifically for Objective-C classes that include Swift header files. Then, I wrapped the above code inside it, which simplified many things.

### @objc & NS_SWIFT_NAME

`@objc`This helps you provide an external definition for Objective-C in your Swift file declarations.

`NS_SWIFT_NAME`Then relative`@objc`They have opposite functions.
